data.title: Phoenix Education Partners: An Interesting Public Offering
data.description:
Shares of Phoenix Education Partners (NYSE:PXED) have seen a convincing move higher on the first day of trading, even as the IPO involved merely shares being sold by selling shareholders. A combination of healthy topline growth, margin expansion, a strong balance sheet and modest valuations leaves shares trade at compelling multiples, leaving me somewhat puzzled.

While this is an attractive opportunity based on healthy numbers, I am mindful of the risks in investing in this sector, given experiences in the past, making me a bit cautious here. I look to learn more about momentum going forward before potentially pulling the trigger here.

A University Built For The Busy & Ambitious

The title header is basically the mission of Phoenix Education Partners, which provides access to higher education opportunities, allowing students to develop knowledge and skills to achieve their own professional goals and provide a valuable supply of talent to organizations.

The company on average has over 80,000 students enrolled so far this year, has over a million alumni with average students having an age in their late-thirties, indicating that it is a popular destination for re-schooling as well. In fact, the company specifically targets such adult learners, those being students older than 25 years of age, which needs are not met by the traditional education system, as these students are often working or have families already.

The university was founded in the mid-seventies and offers 72 degree granting programs across a wide range of disciplines. The vast majority of enrollment is based on bachelor programs, complemented by much lower percentages of master's, associate's and doctoral programs. Degree completions are two-third focused on business and IT, with other much smaller directions including health professions, social & behavioral sciences, education and nursing.

The market of degree-granting and post-secondary institutions covers 19 million students in the US, being a >$800 billion market opportunity. The company particularly targets the adult learner market, as this segment in particular benefits from the rise of online learning opportunities.

Private equity firms Apollo and Vistria acquired the organization in 2017 and sharpened its strategic focus, with the pruchase price of the organization pegged coincidentally at similar valuation as today, nearly a decade later.

Valuation And IPO Thoughts

Phoenix Education Partners aimed to sell 4.25 million shares in a preliminary price range between $31 and $33 per share, with final pricing set at the middle end of the range. Note that all shares were sold by selling shareholders, with no benefits going to the business itself.

With over 35 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded an $1.12 billion equity valuation at the offer price. Unlike many private equity stories, Phoenix operates with a roughly $265 million net cash position, for a roughly $855 million operating asset valuation attached to the firm.

The company posted sound operating financial numbers in the fiscal year ending in August 2023. Operating profits of $82 million on a $835 million revenue number translated into margins near 10% of sales. Revenues rose by 13% to $950 million for the year ending August 2024 as operating profits exploded to $152 million. Growth was driven by an 11% increase in average enrollment numbers to nearly 79,000 students.

Momentum continued in the first nine months of this current fiscal year, with revenues up over 5% to $750 million as operating profits of $150 million translates into margins equal to 20% of sales here! This makes that full year sales are seen over a billion this year, with EBITDA seen up to $243 million as average enrollments so far this year are up 3% to nearly 83,000 students which based on the total revenue number comes down to about $12,000 per student per annum.

Based on the operating profit number of $855 million, operating assets trade at less than 1 times sales, while these revenues carry 20% margins. In fact, based on operating profits around $200 million per annum, the operating earnings multiple only comes to around 4 times, which is puzzling somewhat in light of the growing revenues, although that margin expansion has been very prevalent in recent years.

Shares rose to the $38 mark on the first day of trading, after briefly trading at a high of $47 per share, as the move added just over $200 million in value to the business, with operating assets valued over a billion here. At just a modest premium to the billion mark, valuation discussions remain very reasonable, essentially valuing operating assets equal to sales, with operations valued at 5 times operating profits. This looks very cheap, certainly as its owners are not known for dealing out hand outs to the market.

Concluding Thoughts

Note that this is not the first university to go public, as the risks in investing in education providers are substantial. These include stringent regulation, including accreditation to be provided by the Department of education regarding accreditations. These risks are very real as the university was involved in aggressive recruitment practices and deceptive claims.

This leaves the industry and company subject to claims and litigation, including potential liabilities relating to borrowing programs, political changes in the support for student financing aid programs, etc. etc.

While these risks are very real, I am quite puzzled behind the IPO, certainly that private equity firms have not levered up the business and more so that the valuations are very reasonable by all means. Even if we correct for operating margins essentially doubling in just two years, to be seen at more "normal" levels, earnings multiples remain very reasonable by all means, yet this has been a notoriously difficult sector to invest in, historically.

Amidst this, I am very intrigued but fail to have conviction here given the poor experiences investors have seen investing in the sector in the past, as I look to learn more about the sustainability of current momentum. However, the earnings yields are so compressed that one has to have an interest in the shares here, as I look forward with great interest to learning more about the post-IPO performance, potentially using some more opportunistic funds to get involved here.

data.title: Alaska Airlines: Strong Buy As Market Fails To See The Value (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Alaska Airlines (NYSE:ALK) stock has fallen after the company issued a profit warning for the third quarter. The stock has fallen almost 20% since my last report, underperforming the S&P 500’s 11.6% gain. In this report, I discuss the revised guidance for the third quarter and assess whether this necessitates a change to my price targets and rating.

A Look At Alaska Airlines Profit Warning

The announcement from Alaska Airlines is widely reported as lower profit expectations. However, the company did guide for earnings per share to be between $1.00 and $1.40 and now expects earnings at the lower end of the range. So, the company is not really lowering the guidance range, but it is flagging that the earnings are likely going to be at the lower end of the range. The mid-point of the range was $1.20, it is likely going to be around $1.10 or lower. Analysts had previously expected earnings per share of $1.32 and they are now expecting earnings per share of $1.10.

The company had several drivers for the downward revision. An IT outage in July has a $0.10 impact on earnings and fuel prices were higher than expected. That seems to be a continuing challenge for Alaska Airlines. I have been covering Alaska Airlines for a while and it is not the first time that the company points at oil prices as a drag. This time distributions are the primary cause of the higher economic fuel prices but normally we already see that the company points at structurally higher economic fuel prices in the West Coast.

The good part is that the company signaled strong yield in August and there seem to be no problems specific to Hawaiian Airlines. The company did not alter its full year outlook, but with a downward revision to Q3 that may not be fully off the table. We note that analysts previously expected earnings per share of $3.36 for 2025 and now expect $2.97.

Alaska Airlines Stock Is Now A Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Alaska Airlines, it is definitely the case that expectations have been lowered substantially. EBITDA has been revised down by 18% this year and 8.8% next year. Free cash flow generation has been revised down by a little bit more than a third but up by 15% for 2026. While these revisions are negative, we do note that the company is trading below its median valuation multiple and also below the peer group. The company does face some pressure on West Coast fuel prices and there is some risk regarding the integration of Hawaiian Airlines. However, I do not believe that a 22% discount to the median EV/EBITDA is fully justified for a company that generally is managed well. As a result, I am upgrading the stock to strong buy with a $74.43 price target, indicating 51% upside. We note that the company has around $465 million in repurchase authority remaining under its current share repurchase program. Those remaining share repurchases at current price levels are a driver of upside.

Conclusion: Alaska Airlines Is Mispriced

Alaska Airlines definitely does face pressures on fuel prices, and its IT problems are a headwind in the third quarter with an expected impact for the full year. However, I don’t believe the current stock prices value the stock accurately. We see that neither the 2023 or the 2024 earnings are currently reflected in the stock price which I believe should provide quite some upside. The market just has to stop trading airline stocks in the emotional way that it usually does and assess the fundamentals and supported price targets.

data.title: Calamos Investment Team Outlooks, October 2025
data.description:
By John P. Calamos, Sr., Founder, Chairman and Global Chief Investment Officer

During the third quarter, markets once again surmounted a wall of worry. Although there were many uncertainties, investors ultimately found much to cheer about, from the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill in July to the long-awaited Federal Reserve rate cut in September, to economic data that showed the US economy was resilient and continuing along its growth trajectory. Still-strong corporate profits demonstrated that many businesses were adapting to tariffs and capitalizing on new opportunities, including AI. Additionally, although employment data has softened, the job market has remained stable, and consumers continue to spend.

The economic and market landscape was also positive outside the United States, with many countries benefiting from stimulative monetary and fiscal policies, proving, as our Global Equity Team has noted, that “America First” policies need not preclude growth elsewhere; in fact, they could catalyze opportunities.

Stocks, convertibles and fixed income markets all advanced during the third quarter. As the Fed cut rates, US small caps surged, coming off the heels of a historically long stretch of underperformance relative to large caps. The MSCI Emerging Market Index also posted double-digit gains.

Global Asset Class Performance, Total Return %

Looking forward, we see continued upside for the markets and global economy. Of course, we believe fiscal and monetary policy will be key to driving growth. Certainly, the current US shutdown adds a brick to the proverbial wall of worry.

The Opportunity of Alternatives

In addition to our flagship alternative mutual funds, our ETF lineup offers a variety of choices—from the Calamos Autocallable Income ETF to Structured Alternative Protection portfolios that provide access to the upside potential of equity markets as well as Bitcoin.

We contend that many of the most compelling opportunities for investors will be tied to larger growth themes—whether that’s the evolution of the AI ecosystem, or increased defense spending in Europe. Although market leadership is broadening, this is very much a market where individual security selection will matter and one where we believe our teams can excel. (On that note, see Barron’s recent article “What One Top Small-Cap Growth Fund is Buying Now,” highlighting our small-cap team, led by Senior Portfolio Manager Brandon Nelson, CFA.)

Investors should not be surprised by periods of volatility—whether due to political wrangling, geopolitics, or unexpected data releases. I believe it always pays to be well-diversified, across geographies, market caps, and asset classes. Alternatives can be very helpful on both the equity and fixed income sides of an allocation.

And on the topic of alternatives, I’m pleased to announce that we’ve continued to expand our suite of private market alternative solutions for investors, most recently through the October 1 launch of Calamos Aksia Hedged Strategies Fund. Private markets were once the domain of institutions, and we’re tremendously happy to be able to put these strategies in the hands of individual investors, too.

In Closing

As always, we appreciate the support of the investors and investment professionals who have chosen Calamos Investments, and we’re excited about what the future holds. Below, you’ll find links to commentaries from members of our investment team, where they share their insights about the markets and the opportunities they are finding across asset classes.

Back to the Future

By Michael Grant

The US equity market’s recovery from April lows has been exceptional by any standard. Two explanations dominate: the first is one of US economic resilience led by AI-driven mega-caps, where the profitability divergence between the Magnificent Seven and the broader corporate world has defined this decade. Here, the central question remains timing the peak of investor euphoria around this super-cycle.

The second explanation concerns the revisionism of Donald Trump. Throughout the spring, most assumed his agenda would bring disruption. These fears weren’t mistaken, but the emphasis was misplaced: Trump 2.0 represents multifaceted reflation rather than disruption for the US economy. His latest hunger for Fed acquiescence adds another tailwind. We emphasize this distinction—reflation versus risk—because Trump’s polarizing presence creates a polemical dust storm that can obscure fundamentals.

Our anticipation has been for late summer weakness in equities to create buying opportunities ahead of an S&P 500 rally toward 7,000 in Q1 2026. The problem: this is virtually consensus thinking. Investors have fallen over themselves seeking pullbacks to buy. Meanwhile, the crosscurrents multiply. Financial liquidity appears abundant, while economic liquidity feels tight. The Federal Reserve has again been proven “behind the curve,” and an incipient growth scare has emerged.

AI Everything

Our largest worry is the extraordinary over-investment and over-enthusiasm surrounding AI. The scale of today’s mania upon activity and profitability is undeniable and sets it apart from the dot-com boom. Hyperscaler capex is running at an annual rate of $400 billion and is estimated to reach a cumulative total well north of $3 trillion by decade’s end—equivalent to 10% of US GDP. To cover this cost of capital, annual sales exceeding $3 trillion must emerge from somewhere.

Consider the scale: OpenAI (OPENAI) generated a paltry $4 billion in revenue in the first half of 2025. And yet, Oracle’s >$317 billion increase in RPO backlog* in the latest quarter (largely driven by OpenAI) was 70% of the quarterly increase in US GDP. These companies are sinking trillions into AI because it represents a classic Innovator’s Dilemma: today’s new technologies will likely drain the competitive moats surrounding their extremely profitable, monopoly-like businesses.

For now, this spending enjoys a positive feedback loop between rising investment and rising profits. Those selling AI infrastructure enjoy profits in full, while buyers depreciate expenses far into the future rather than the present. This is viable because these businesses possess monopoly-like characteristics. Until the “losers of AI” are understood, the party continues. What remains to be seen is what level of returns will come out of all this spending.

The likelihood is that AI will either create more losers than winners among today’s technology leaders, or the incremental revenue opportunity is mightily overhyped, pointing to a spending collapse. Both scenarios imply an inevitable transition in investor mood, yet timing this shift is hard. The most likely catalyst may be growing awareness of the limitations of the newer training models—ChatGPT-5, for example, is a dud.

Until then, investors will run with the herd—as banks did ahead of the Global Financial Crisis and as European mobile companies did in the late 1990s when they grossly overbid for 3G licenses. The pattern is familiar even if the particulars differ. Much of this is uncontroversial, but recognizing a mania doesn’t immunize against its continuation. Still, it should be a cautionary signal for portfolio concentration.

The Agitation That Never Rests

This narrative of rising secular risk for the dominant but concentrated part of the equity world is conjoined by a second (and positive) narrative: multifaceted reflation for the broader economy after several years of profit recession. Here, investment advisors must filter a deluge of overexcited and overpoliticized commentary. Apprehension about the stagflationary consequences of Trump’s revisionist agenda has proven exaggerated.

While a few segments of the US economy are under stress, major macroeconomic figures have unfolded largely according to trend. The inflationary repercussions of tariffs are barely perceptible. Meanwhile, Trump has removed the roadblock of Fed intransigence. The recommencement of easing by the Federal Reserve, combined with the administration’s focus on housing, suggests the broader economy still has support.

In the absence of a dramatic end to the AI spending boom, some spillover from technology to nontechnology areas of the economy points to revived leadership by cyclical parts of the market. Put simply, there is more to a business than a GPU. This creates opportunity even as mega-cap concentration creates risk. The investment challenge lies in maintaining exposure to both narratives while managing their distinct and differing risk profiles.

Outlook

Equities need US economic activity to moderate just enough to support Fed rate cuts, but not so much as to strain corporate earnings. Global growth appears resilient while inflation remains subdued. Concerns about global trade are fading, while AI euphoria could persist into early 2026. The removal of Fed Chair Powell in late spring will be discounted as a backstop for economic activity.

By that time, much of the good news will be discounted amid unsustainable AI assumptions and the likelihood of a more difficult economic setting in 2027. Today’s setup argues for balanced positioning: rising wariness toward AI-driven momentum stocks given valuation extremes and sustainability, but healthy exposure to cyclicals on the assumption of sustained US expansion into 2026.

* Remaining performance obligations (RPO) backlog is the total value of contracted revenue a company hasn’t yet recognized but is obligated to deliver.

Steady Performance Through Shifting Terrain

By Jason Hill

In contrast to the second quarter, when markets fought through a dramatic selloff following “Liberation Day” tariff announcements to claw their way to new highs, the third quarter was less volatile. Investor sentiment was more upbeat as Fed easing came into focus and data pointed to continued economic resilience.

The Fed delivered its first rate cut in September, lowering rates by 0.25% with additional cuts projected for the remainder of the year. This policy pivot, driven largely by softening labor market data, has not prompted us to change our approach.

Interestingly, broad index volatility remained flat and historically on the lower side for the quarter, yet single-name volatility persisted. This environment of calm markets with active individual stock movement provided ideal conditions for the fund’s strategies, particularly in convertible arbitrage, where the fund benefits from company-specific price action without broad market disruption.

CMNIX performed well during the quarter, delivering returns consistent with traditional fixed income while maintaining the reduced volatility and interest-rate risk that investors seek from bond alternatives. The strategy’s steady performance contrasted favorably with the more unpredictable moves in traditional bond markets as yields fluctuated around Fed communications and economic data.

The Case for CMNIX: Outperforming Bonds over the Long Term

All four sleeves of the fund (hedged equity, convertible arbitrage, special purpose acquisition company (SPAC) arbitrage, and merger arbitrage) posted positive performance over the three quarters of the year. Our convertible arbitrage book and hedged equity strategy had similar gross returns in the third quarter, although the ride in the hedged equity sleeve was a bit bumpier.

How We Are Positioned

We believe one of the fund’s key advantages is our flexibility to adjust our allocations on an ongoing basis, versus funds with more static rebalancing strategies. That said, the most significant change during the quarter was an increased allocation to convertible arbitrage, funded by a modest reduction in the hedged equity sleeve. This reallocation reflected the extraordinary new issuance environment that has developed over the first three quarters of the year.

CMNIX Allocation

Numbers may not total 100% due to rounding.

Hedged Equity. This sleeve is positioned to provide upside participation in the equity market, while seeking to limit downside exposure. Our hedged equity allocation decreased modestly as we redirected capital toward convertible opportunities, but we simultaneously enhanced our defensive positioning with the addition of long puts out to year-end and continued to opportunistically layer in risk-reducing put spreads, maintaining downside risk mitigation while preserving modest upside participation.

The hedge remains toward the more defensive end of its historical range, reflecting the current opportunity set and our view that while markets may continue higher, there are potential volatility catalysts ahead.

Arbitrage. Convertible arbitrage is the core allocation of CMNIX’s arbitrage sleeve, with opportunistic allocations to other strategies, such as merger arbitrage and SPAC arbitrage.

We increased the fund’s convertible arbitrage weighting during the quarter, driven by exceptional new issuance opportunities. The US convertible market saw over $33 billion in new deals during the third quarter—a dramatic increase from the roughly $10 billion issued in the third quarter of 2024. For the year-to-date, US convertible issuance is on a pace to beat 2001’s record issuance of $106 billion. Global year-to-date issuance has already surpassed full-year issuance for 2024 and is also in striking distance of 2020 and 2021. This increased issuance expands the opportunity set and has refreshed the convertible market, providing opportunities for pricing arbitrage as issuers compete for investor dollars.

Equally important, the secondary convertible market demonstrated remarkable health, easily absorbing this heavy new supply without pressure on existing paper. This ability to digest substantial new issuance while maintaining market stability signals a robust, liquid convertible ecosystem—precisely the conditions that support our core strategy.

Given our expectation of continued global economic growth and increased clarity around policy in the US, we expect to see more companies access the convertible market to fund growth or refinance existing debt. Even if interest rates continue to come down, we believe the current environment incentivizes issuers to contain borrowing costs by choosing convertibles over nonconvertible debt. And while not a lock by any means, we’re not ruling out a “green swan” burst of new convertible issuance in the coming years.

Global Convertible Issuance is Surging ($ bil)

Merger arbitrage and SPAC arbitrage are highly idiosyncratic strategies, and our allocations to both have held steady over the quarter and since the start of the year. In the coming months, we expect the Trump administration will focus more attention on lowering regulatory hurdles, which could lead to more opportunities in merger arbitrage.

Meanwhile, we have seen an uptick in SPAC new issuance, albeit off a very low base. Although we have been active in these deals, this activity hasn’t materially increased the fund’s overall level of SPAC exposure.

Conclusion

The third quarter reinforced our conviction that CMNIX serves its intended role as a bond alternative capable of generating steady returns across varying market conditions. Our tactical approach allowed us to capitalize on exceptional convertible market conditions while the hedged equity book helped maintain the defensive characteristics that define our strategy.

Looking ahead, with the S&P 500 Index near all-time highs and additional Fed rate cuts anticipated, we see multiple potential volatility catalysts that could create opportunities for our strategy. Whether from concerns about excessive AI spending, ongoing geopolitical tensions, or evolving tariff policies, more uncertainty likely lies ahead. We believe our dual-core strategy approach (arbitrage and hedged equity) and flexible positioning will continue to serve investors well in this environment. The convertible market’s demonstrated ability to handle heavy issuance while maintaining healthy secondary trading, paired with our defensively positioned hedged equity strategy, positions us well for whatever the final quarter may bring.

Global Convertibles Extend Their Winning Streak Through 3Q

By Eli Pars, CFA

Review

During the third quarter, the global convertible market extended its unusually strong run. The FTSE Global Convertibles Index returned 9.13%, outpacing the MSCI World Index, up 7.36%. For the year-to-date through September, the global convertible market’s gain of 21.33% now exceeds the return of the global equity market by 350 basis points.

In July, we noted that one likely driver for global convertibles’ atypical outperformance was the reversal of fortunes in the Magnificent Seven mega-caps and a broadening of market leadership. This quarter, convertibles also benefited from the excitement surrounding the AI infrastructure buildout. The rally in US small caps that followed the Federal Reserve’s much-hoped-for interest rate cut also provided a nice tailwind. (As we discussed in our whitepaper, “The Case for Strategic Convertible Allocations,” the global convertible market has materially different market capitalization characteristics than broad equity market benchmarks like the MSCI World Index. Although diversified across market caps, small- and mid-caps represent the lion’s share of convertible issuers, while mega-caps are scarcer.)

Calamos Global Convertible Fund (CXGCX) also posted a strong return, while maintaining what we believe is a better risk-reward profile with greater focus on downside risk management and less exposure to speculative names. For the quarter, the fund’s gain of 8.92% captured more than 97% of the FTSE Global Convertibles Index’s 9.13% advance, and more than 121% of the MSCI World Index’s upside. For the year-to-date, the fund’s 18.85% return captured 88% of the global convertible market’s upside and nearly 106% of the MSCI World Index’s 17.83% gain.

Issuance

Performance wasn’t the only unusually strong characteristic of the convertible market during the quarter. Issuance also surged. Led by US companies, which brought more than $33 billion in new paper to market, global convertible issuance totaled more than $54 billion for the quarter. For the year, global issuance totals more than $125 billion, surpassing year-to-date issuance for the past three years, and on track to beat 2001’s high-water mark of $167 billion. Although issuance has been diversified across sectors, names tied to AI infrastructure buildout have been especially well represented; a number of these issuers also have roots in cryptocurrency mining. Meanwhile, although there has been an uptick in investment-grade issuance, we haven’t seen the ramp-up that we thought would be needed to reach current levels.

New issuance at these levels is certainly eye-catching. That said, it pays to be selective. While many of the issues that have recently come to market offer attractive total return features (an attractive balance of upside equity participation and risk mitigation), we’re comfortable passing on others. For example, in line with Calamos Global Convertible Fund’s focus on lower-volatility equity participation, we are cautious of convertibles with more speculative attributes and excessive exposure to equity market downside. As a result, we’ve chosen to limit our cryptocurrency exposure to companies with stronger fundamentals—those we’d consider to be the “blue chips of crypto.” Similarly, we’re concerned about signs of speculative excess in AI datacenters, which has led us to position our datacenter exposure more defensively than the benchmarks.

Global convertible issuance is soaring in 2025

While we would not be surprised to see the extraordinary pace of convertible issuance moderate from here, there are many reasons it could continue at a brisk clip. Even in a lower interest rate environment, convertibles’ cost-savings benefits (i.e., lower borrowing costs) make them a compelling choice for companies seeking to refinance debt (convertible or otherwise) or fund growth initiatives.

Positioning

We continue to emphasize total-return convertibles. On average, the fund’s portfolio has a slightly higher delta than the convertible market, while maintaining what we believe is a better risk-reward profile.

We are bottom-up investors, and our allocations to sectors and regions result from individual security selection decisions, not top-down calls. With that said, as of the end of the quarter, the fund’s largest allocations were to the consumer discretionary and information technology sectors, followed by industrials and health care. Within the fund’s consumer discretionary allocation, Chinese online retailers and companies involved in travel and tourism are well-represented. Regarding the former, we pared exposure during the quarter, taking some chips off the table to lock in gains.

From a geographic perspective, the fund’s largest country allocation is to the United States, followed by China and Japan. As noted above, our allocation to China has come down over the quarter as a result of bottom-up trades. From a broader regional perspective, we remain overweight to Emerging Asia and to North America. We’re maintaining our significant underweight to Europe despite the growing buzz about an improving macro environment; we believe the risk-reward and fundamentals of individual issuers are less unappealing than what we’re finding elsewhere.

Conclusion

Economic conditions may not be booming but they aren’t contracting either. Easing monetary policy and stimulative fiscal policy will likely be enough to maintain a slow growth trajectory and provide a favorable backdrop for equities and convertibles. Corporate profits have been resilient, reflecting businesses’ ability to adapt to tariffs and other disruptions. While we are yet to be convinced that near-term AI capex will meet the lofty heights that some forecast, we believe it can provide an upside tailwind to economic growth. That said, there are still political and geopolitical risks that we can’t ignore, and we are wary of getting caught up in speculative pockets of the convertible market. Instead, we believe our emphasis on total return convertibles issued by fundamentally strong companies will continue to serve Calamos Global Convertible Fund well.

Steady Course Delivers: Maintaining Conviction with AI Innovation and Infrastructure

By John Hillenbrand, CPA

As we enter the fourth quarter of 2025, we believe our steady-course approach has continued to be validated. Rather than chasing momentum or dramatically shifting positioning, we’ve maintained focus on our core investment thesis: the transformative power of AI innovation, the critical infrastructure buildout supporting it, the resilience of quality companies, and the value of multi-asset diversification.

We believe this consistency, combined with selective adjustments, has been an important contributor to positive year-to-date performance. Calamos Growth and Income Fund (CGIIX) has gained 15.27% year-to-date through September 30, 2025, surpassing the Morningstar Moderately Aggressive Allocation Category return of 11.08%, as well as the S&P 500 Index return of 14.83%.

Economic Foundation Remains Solid

The US economy continues to demonstrate resilience despite evolving dynamics. Consumer spending maintains momentum at 2.5%‒3.0% real growth, primarily driven by higher-income households, which account for over 40% of total consumption and continue to benefit from wealth effects.

Meanwhile, the infrastructure supercycle continues to accelerate across multiple dimensions. Capital spending remains robust, with hyperscaler capex expectations up over 30% since January, reflecting the massive buildout of AI infrastructure. Simultaneously, reshoring initiatives drive the construction of manufacturing facilities, while federal infrastructure programs support transportation, energy grid modernization, and broadband expansion. This multi-year investment cycle creates opportunities across industrials, materials, and engineering firms while supporting sustained economic growth.

The labor market remains fundamentally healthy despite a moderation in job growth. Immigration policy shifts—with net immigration dropping from 3 million annually in 2022‒2024 to near zero—represent a significant structural change that could tighten labor markets and support wage growth, particularly for construction and infrastructure-related jobs.

AI Transformation: Accelerating Value Creation

The artificial intelligence revolution has entered the deployment phase with clear value creation emerging. Businesses with high AI exposure are rapidly adopting these tools—45% of US businesses now have paid AI subscriptions, predominantly for productivity and coding applications.

AI’s impacts span the Calamos Growth and Income Fund portfolio through enhanced productivity in enterprise applications, emerging direct revenue streams, and margin expansion through AI-optimized operations. Critically, this transformation drives unprecedented infrastructure demand—not just for semiconductors and data centers, but also for power generation, cooling systems, and the broader ecosystem of companies supporting this buildout. While depreciation from massive infrastructure investments will partially offset margin gains, we focus on companies effectively monetizing AI as well as those providing essential infrastructure components.

Consumer Divergence Requires Selectivity

The consumer presents a nuanced picture. Top-quintile earners show the strongest wage growth, reversing post-pandemic compression trends. Combined with wealth appreciation, this cohort drives spending momentum.

Lower-income consumers face headwinds from potential tariff impacts, reduced social support, and restarting student loans, although stronger-than-usual balance sheets provide some cushion. The net impact appears manageable but requires monitoring.

Portfolio Positioning: Proven Themes

Our investment approach emphasizes:

  • Technology leadership—companies with clear AI monetization paths, including hyperscalers and software companies, seeing rapid AI-enhanced product adoption
  • Infrastructure beneficiaries—firms positioned to benefit from the multi-year infrastructure supercycle, including datacenter REITs, power generation, industrial equipment manufacturers, and engineering companies supporting both AI and traditional infrastructure buildout
  • Quality growth—businesses exposed to affluent consumers and those benefiting from secular trends, including housing demand
  • Financial strength—banks benefiting from regulatory normalization and yield curve dynamics, particularly those with exposure to infrastructure project financing
  • Risk management—convertible securities providing equity participation with downside protection, while options strategies add income and volatility management

Risk Management in Focus

We acknowledge key risks requiring careful monitoring:

  • Valuation extremes with market pricing increasingly dependent on growth materializing in 2027 and beyond, while momentum readings echo late-1999
  • Execution gaps between AI investment and monetization
  • Labor market uncertainty from AI automation and immigration changes
  • External factors, including trade relationships and credit conditions

Looking Forward with Disciplined Optimism

We believe our steady course has proven its worth through multiple market environments this year. We do not chase momentum; rather, our consistent focus on AI innovation leaders and high-quality companies, through our multi-asset approach, has delivered solid results. The AI revolution remains in its early stages, and companies that successfully navigate this transformation should capture disproportionate value creation.

We maintain our constructive but risk-aware stance. Current valuations increasingly reflect growth expectations extending well into the next decade, demanding continued discipline, but we see no reason to abandon proven themes. Our multi-asset structure, which combines growth equity exposure with convertibles and options overlays, provides flexibility to capture opportunities while managing downside risk. We believe this balanced approach positions Calamos Growth and Income Fund to navigate both near-term volatility and long-term opportunities as the investment landscape continues to evolve.

Global Markets: Diverging Paths, Expanding Opportunities

By Nick Niziolek, CFA, Dennis Cogan, CFA, Paul Ryndak, CFA and Kyle Ruge, CFA

We believe the case for global and international allocations continues to strengthen, supported by exceptional opportunities around the world.

Over recent months, we’ve seen broad gains and moderate volatility, supported by easing monetary policy and fiscal stimulus in many parts of the world. Rising earnings and corporate profits demonstrate that companies are adapting to change and disruption, whether that’s global tariffs, the evolution of the AI ecosystem, or growth opportunities specific to countries and regions.

As we entered the year, many investors worried that “America First” policies and a move away from globalization would exact a steep toll on overseas markets and economies, but the opposite has proven true. Indeed, the policy agenda coming out of Washington has provided catalysts for economies worldwide to move forward with their own pro-growth initiatives, creating many exciting opportunities for investors. Germany’s defense industry is a prime example.

Year-to-date returns across global markets illustrate the breadth of opportunity that has emerged. Many countries and regions are trading at or near multi-year or all-time highs—including Europe, the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, Japan, and select emerging markets, across capitalizations. We’ve seen the dollar move lower to sideways across most currency pairs, and valuation and interest rate differentials point to further downside, which in turn would support non-US markets.

In 2025, there has been an opportunity cost for home-country bias

Global Asset Class Performance

Year-to-date total return through 9/30/25 (USD, %)

As we look forward, we see many reasons for optimism—and continued upside for many areas of global equities. Earnings growth estimates for the next year are more positive in more places over the next year. Valuations are also attractive in many overseas markets.

As a wider set of opportunities emerges across regions, our team is:

  • Emphasizing companies across a mix of cohorts. Secular growth names continue to provide the foundation of our portfolios. We are also increasing participation in cyclical growth, particularly within financials and industrials and boosting commodities exposure. Our positioning in defensive names is more selective; the opportunities are less attractive.
  • Bullish on Europe and other overseas developed markets. Lower inflation and moderate growth supported by fiscal stimulus provide a favorable environment, including in Germany, the UK, Italy, Spain, the Netherlands and Canada. We’re investing in leaders in technology infrastructure and ecommerce, financials, defense & aerospace, and specialized industrials.
  • Finding many opportunities in emerging markets. Central bank easing, a weaker dollar, and reduced policy uncertainty create a better backdrop for many emerging markets, including China, India, Brazil, Mexico and South Korea. Key themes in our emerging market positions include the AI supply chain, evolving trade relationships (e.g., friendshoring and reshoring beneficiaries), and EM-consumer plays.
  • Encouraged by improvements in Japan. These include a return to higher nominal growth, improved corporate governance, and capital efficiency. We’re finding particularly compelling growth potential in industrials and AI infrastructure, semiconductors and equipment, ecommerce, and media.
  • Continuing to identify opportunities in the United States. Easing monetary policy, a respectable job market, continued consumer spending (albeit with significant bifurcation by income cohort), tax cuts and AI capex support expectations that the US economy can continue to expand, sustaining equity opportunities.
  • Using our proven approach to capitalize on opportunities while managing risks. Our approach combines top-down thematic analysis with rigorous capital structure research and draws on experience earned over multiple market cycles and market regimes.

Calamos Global and International Funds

Our global and international funds share the same experienced team and time-tested process; their different risk/reward profiles and investment universes support diverse asset allocation goals.

Fund Opportunity set Our focus

Calamos Global Opportunities Fund (CGCIX)
  • Developed and emerging markets
  • Equities and convertible securities; all-cap flexibility
  • Equity-like returns with less volatility and favorable skew
  • Companies with higher quality attributes, growth orientation

Calamos Global Equity Fund (CIGEX)
  • Developed and emerging markets countries
  • Equities; all-cap flexibility
  • Participation in quality growth companies from around the world.

Calamos Evolving World Growth Fund (CNWIX)
  • Emerging-market companies and select developed market-domiciled businesses with significant revenue exposures attributable to emerging markets
  • Equities and opportunistic use of convertibles; all-cap flexibility
  • Harnessing growth potential of emerging markets through a risk-aware approach

Calamos International Growth Fund (CIGIX)
  • Developed (ex US) and emerging markets
  • Equities, all-cap flexibility
  • Rapidly growing, higher quality international companies

Calamos International Small Cap Growth Fund (CSGIX)
  • Developed (ex-US) and emerging markets
  • Companies with superior earnings growth potential and demand tailwinds, providing diversification benefits away from US multinationals.

A Precariously Concentrated Top 10 Makes the Case for Quality

By Jim Madden, CFA, Tony Tursich, CFA and Beth Williamson

Good news? Bad news? No news? It didn’t matter since nothing was able to stop stocks’ inexorable march upward. The S&P 500 Index is now more than 30% off its April lows and near all-time highs.

With valuations also near historic highs, what can we expect going forward? Certainly, new highs are possible, but we believe closer inspection is now revealing that the economy and the stock market are showing signs of vulnerability.

First, we have a very narrow stock market, with 10 stocks driving over half the market capitalization gain since 2021. Perhaps even more worrisome is that nine of these 10 stocks are essentially AI-related.

In addition, we have a very narrow economy, with 10% of earners now responsible for 50% of US spending. This is an all-time high, with asset owners benefitting from unprecedented Fed liquidity and suppressed interest rates following the Great Financial Crisis.

So, we have an economy based on the spending of the top 10%, and those top 10% are highly dependent on the stock market. Meanwhile, we have a stock market highly dependent on 10 stocks, and those 10 stocks are highly dependent on possible returns on AI spending.

As long-time quality investors, the Sustainable Equities team has watched quality lag for the past four-plus years. What does the current setup we described above mean for quality stocks going forward? While there are a range of outcomes and it’s impossible to predict the timing of any market disruption, here are two scenarios we consider to be more likely:

  1. All the spending on AI starts to see a return as it broadens out across the economy. This would be positive for quality stocks, given that they have shown the ability to adapt, innovate and deal with the kind of disruption AI will bring. With that history, we feel quality stocks have the best chance to benefit from AI efficiencies.
  2. If the massive spending on AI infrastructure does not produce expected returns, we would expect to see a slowdown in the earnings of the top 10 stocks. This slowdown would ripple across the economy and likely result in a market correction and increased volatility. This type of market scenario favors quality stocks as investors seek the safety of consistent performers.

While our quality emphasis, valuation discipline, and diversification across sectors and positions have not been aligned with market sentiment, we believe the top-heavy nature of the market and economy strengthens the case for our approach.

data.title: McKesson: Strategic Shift To High-Growth Oncology And Prescription Tech Signals Long Opportunity
data.description:
I put a ‘Buy’ label on McKesson Corporation’s (NYSE:MCK) stock after observing its transformation into a higher-growth + higher-margin healthcare-services-platform. My thesis is mostly focused on McKesson's strategic realignment towards its Oncology & Multispecialty and Prescription Technology Solutions segments. The bullish change that emerged from the realignment can be observed in a newly raised long-term adjusted EPS growth target of 13%-16%. The EPS growth is based on a flywheel effect in oncology. Here (in oncology), scale in distribution and GPO services uplift the clinical trial data and expand biopharma deals. So, the margin expansion is coming from high-value access and affordability solutions. McKesson's solid FCF generation supports a capital allocation strategy that directly improves value under aggressive stock repurchases and constant dividend growth.

Regulatory pressure on U.S. drug pricing is the primary risk that I marked. It can compress margins in McKesson's main growth segments. Additional risks (that I consider) include execution risk in integrating large-scale acquisitions [like Core Ventures and PRISM], lingering opioid litigation overhangs, and high customer concentration. MCK stock's strong performance [total return] with the fundamentals attached, I view any possible market-based price pullbacks as accumulation opportunities for a long position. The long position can capitalize on the durable growth that is not yet fully priced into McKesson stock’s legacy valuations.

McKesson’s Upgraded EPS Guidance and Strategic Realignment Signal Solid Long-Term Growth

I observed that the most direct driver for MCK stock performance is the upgraded financial guidance and long-term targets. Following the strong Q1-FY2026, McKesson raised its full-year adjusted EPS guidance to a range of $37.1 to $37.9. This initial guidance was more refined and uplifted to $38.05 to $38.55 during its 2025 Investor Day. This shift in EPS outlook is a clear signal of the forward business's progress. This updated forecast indicates a 15% to 17% Y/Y EPS growth. The growth is 14% to 17% excluding prior-year gains from McKesson Ventures. More fundamentally important is the upward revision of McKesson's long-term adjusted EPS growth target to 13% to 16% and it is up from the previous 12% to 14%. This long-term target signifies a detailed segment-specific growth strategy. To add credibility here, Analyst consensus estimates reflect this optimism, with EPS projected to grow at a double-digit CAGR through FY2030 by attaining a consensus of $64.05. This constant upward revision of both near-term and long-term targets provides the stock with a clear base of forward earnings power. This is a primary determinant of stock valuation. Now, why am I stating this? See the stock reaction on the Investor Day [September 23, 2025].

I want to be direct and specific here. The optimistic outlook is based on McKesson's strategic transformation and portfolio realignment that has sharpened its focus on high-growth + high-margin businesses. For instance, the recent announcement of a new organizational structure [effective in Q2-FY2026] is a main forward catalyst. The creation of distinct segments for "North American Pharmaceutical" and "Oncology & Multispecialty" builds high transparency into McKesson's growth factors. Earlier, the high-growth oncology business was embedded within the U.S. Pharmaceutical segment. But now, as a standalone unit, the market can more clearly model McKesson's performance. The long-term adjusted operating profit growth targets for these new core segments are important to note. These are 13% to 16% for Oncology & Multispecialty and 10% to 13% for Prescription Technology Solutions. Also, this strategic shift is along with the divestiture of lower-margin [strategically misaligned] assets, like the exit from the European market with the sale of its Norway businesses and the separation of the Medical-Surgical segment. So, the portfolio optimization streamlines ops and leads McKesson to concentrate capital and resources on areas with the highest potential for value.

McKesson's Oncology & Multispecialty and RxTS Segments Drive Margin Expansion for FY2026

As I have assessed, the newly formed Oncology & Multispecialty segment can be an advanced driver of bottom-line growth. McKesson has built an integrated platform that creates a flywheel effect. The acquisitions of Core Ventures (Florida Cancer Specialists) and PRISM Vision have expanded this network to ~3,300 providers in oncology and over 180 providers in the burgeoning retina and ophthalmology. This scale creates a cycle that drives distribution volume and GPO purchasing power, that in turn scales patient access to clinical trials. This scale may continue to derive valuable real-world data through its Ontada platform that can then be commercialized with biopharma partners. The addressable market for this segment is big, with the community oncology market alone holding $115 billion. The projected adjusted operating profit growth of 49% to 53% for this segment in FY2026 [improved by recent acquisitions] marks its bottom-line possibilities and its role as a primary base for McKesson's forward EPS growth.

Similarly, the Prescription Technology Solutions (RxTS) segment is also a major source of forward margin expansion and scaling of profitability. The segment addresses complex issues in medication access and affordability that represent a market opportunity of $19 billion. In Q1-FY2026, the segment had strong operating leverage with a 16% revenue jump and a 21% increase in operating profit. The access solutions portion [25% of RxTS revenue] of this business derived 65% of the segment's adjusted operating profit. The bottom-line advantage indicates the access solutions’ high-margin nature. The increasing prevalence of high-cost specialty drugs, like the booming GLP-1 market (which already contributes 11% of RxTS's revenue), forms a durable tailwind for RxTS. McKesson's investments in tech [the acquisitions of RxLightning and FastAuth] to digitize patient enrollment and prior authorizations solidify its market advantage. The updated FY2026 guidance raised RxTS’s adjusted operating profit growth forecast from 9%-13% to 11%-15% that confirms the progress in this high-growth + high-margin business, possible EPS scaling, and the following valuation uplift of MCK stock.

McKesson’s Strategic Sharpness and Capital Allocation to Drive Big Stock Price Growth by FY2030

In brief, McKesson's strategic efforts drive considerable operating leverage. The ratio of OpEx to gross profit improved by ~1,200-basis-points since FY2021. The efficiency is coming from cost controls and strategic investments in automation and tech across its distribution network. Here, new distribution centers are attaining up to 90% automation. This operational advantage directly leads to strong FCF that is predictable. McKesson's FCF may end up between $4.4 billion and $4.8 billion in FY2026. This cash generation scales McKesson’s capital allocation. McKesson has a history of constant return of capital, and now a $2.5 billion stock repurchase in FY2026 is projected [with a remaining authorization of $6.9 billion as of June 2025]. This aggressive buyback program has materially reduced the share count and boosted EPS (and with that improved PE valuations).

Further, McKesson's dividend payments had a recent 15% increase and, with that, nine consecutive years of increases. So, this configuration of strategic growth investment and direct capital returns, backed by a strong bottom line, can continue to lift the stock price higher. Based on 10-Y forward PE and PS cap levels [on these levels, the multiples are hitting resistance] of 20x and 0.225x along with consensus EPS and revenue estimates [from Seeking Alpha], the MCK stock can have a 69.5% and 31.4% positive shift in stock price and market cap by FY2030 [apart from dividend yield].

To verify this with the technical analysis, I assessed MCK stock’s weekly price chart. The price trendlines (grey lines) confirmed this positive momentum with a decisive upward channel. There is a buying pressure at the modified blended quarterly EMAs (green and white lines) that is leading to breakouts of resistance. Projecting the trendlines over the Fibonacci levels provides $909 as an optimistic price projection for MCK stock by 2025’s end [based on the long-term trendlines on higher-highs (originated in 2021-2022 and stabilized on EMAs) and the expectation of a solid Santa Claus rally]. However, using the average momentum [trendlines over higher-highs] $817 price for MCK stock is possible on the upside. If the bullish trend does not progress as expected $715 and $686 are the important support levels in the short-term. As these levels are in line with the pivot of the horizontal price channel and the lower bound of it crossing with trendlines at higher-lows.

Regulatory, Legal, and Customer Risks in McKesson's Growth Strategy

McKesson's major external threat is in the shifting regulatory and political grounds on drug pricing in the U.S. The potential impact of policies like the Big Beautiful Bill, potential cuts to Medicaid, and changes to ASP-based reimbursement models may not be material in the short term. But these issues represent a continued long-term risk. McKesson's high-growth Oncology & Multispecialty segment relies on provider-administered specialty drugs. So, it is mainly sensitive to any reforms that can compress provider margins and incentivize a shift in care away from the community setting [that McKesson services]. Although McKesson is actively engaged in advocacy in Washington, D.C., the outcome of future drug pricing legislation is a big uncertainty that can negatively impact the profitability of its most strategic areas of growth. Any adverse policy change (mainly on pricing) can lead to a re-rating of the stock as the market adjusts McKesson's long-term margin projection.

Additionally, ongoing litigation and contingent liabilities specifically related to the distribution of controlled substances are also a material risk [financial and reputational]. For instance, McKesson had a total litigation liability of $6.377 billion related to opioid claims as of June 2025. A large portion of this is related to established settlement agreements. However, recent SEC 10Q filings state that McKesson "is not able to conclude that a liability is probable or provide a reasonable estimate for the range of ultimate possible loss for opioid-related litigation matters other than those for which an accrual is described." [Page 25] This means there can be potential additional unquantified financial exposure from ongoing or forward claims from non-settling parties. This can result in further charges to the bottom-line and cash outflows. It is obvious, but I am marking this because a persistent headline risk with these legal issues can act as a factor for a sudden price drop in MCK stock [sentiment-based, even in the absence of new financial charges].

Moreover, customer concentration and credit risk are a threat too for McKesson's bottom-line stability. The recent bankruptcy of Rite Aid can be seen as a reminder of this. In Q1-FY2026, McKesson had a $189 million pre-tax provision for bad debts related to Rite Aid's second bankruptcy. [Although management has downplayed its ongoing impact, I do not agree with them.] This event directly reduced GAAP earnings and indicates the financial consequences of a big customer default. As per McKesson's 10-K, its single largest customer, CVS Health, holds ~24% of consolidated revenues in FY2025. I observed that this relationship is long-standing and strategic. But the heavy dependence on a single customer for nearly a quarter of the topline creates a large concentration risk. Any adverse change in this relationship, whether through contract renegotiation at less favorable terms and or a strategic shift by CVS toward self-distribution, can have a material adverse effect on McKesson's topline and profitability.

McKesson's Growth May Face Execution Risks and Potential Price Correction

Back to the main argument, McKesson's growth strategy logically has execution risk. The integration of its major acquisitions [Core Ventures and PRISM Vision] is important to attain the projected 49% to 53% operating profit growth in the Oncology & Multispecialty segment (Investor-Day-Transcript). Business-wise, integrating large complex provider networks can have operational issues, and any failure to realize expected synergies and retain main providers can lead to underperformance relative to current expectations. Along with that, the planned separation of the Medical-Surgical business is a major corporate move. The process clearly involves complex audited carve-out financials, the establishment of transition services agreements, and is subject to market conditions for a good IPO/spin-off. The timeline for the full exit can extend to H2-2027. Any delays, unforeseen large costs, and a poor market reception for the new entity can distract resources and fail to derive the expected value. This can lead to market disappointment and stock price correction as a result.

Finally, technical indicators like recent Relative Strength Index [RSI] divergence and market sentiment suggest that the stock may be vulnerable to a correction. The YTD Total Return Performance of MCK stock has outperformed its benchmarks decisively with a total return of 32.05%. The 14-Day RSI reflects that the stock has spent considerable time in an overbought state in early 2025 and in recent weeks [RSI value frequently above 70 and built up short-term divergence]. This technical signal suggests that the stock's upward momentum may be overextended, and as seen in the technical chart above, the MCK stock entered a resistance zone that makes the market tilted towards profit-taking that can lead to a potential pullback in the near term. I am saying a RSI can move upwards from its current neutral state, and a high RSI [>70] can last in a strong uptrend, but an elevated risk of short-term volatility (should not be ignored) as MCK stock is considerably far from 200-Day EMA [also my model attached above suggests a negative shift in FY2026 theoretical stock price and market cap.

__

Overall, my thesis on McKesson stock is inclined on the Bullish side. The strategic clarity and proven bottom-line growth [~17% Adjusted EPS CAGR (FY21 to FY26F)] and capital allocation [dividends + buybacks] are bases for capital appreciation in the upcoming years. Although the stock may seem to be overextended, any market/sentiment-based pullback can be considered as an opportunity to go long. To keep a check on my MCK stock thesis, I will actively monitor direct performance indicators and strategic milestones under McKesson's guidance and strategic plans.

These are Execution Against Raised Financial Targets [whether McKesson's annual adjusted EPS growth remains within or exceeds the newly raised long-term target, the performance of the newly realigned core segments against their stated long-term adjusted operating profit growth targets, managerial commentary on organic growth versus acquisition contributions in quarterly earnings calls, margin performance, and growth in high-value access solutions and FCF conversion rates]. On the Strategic Portfolio Transformation Milestones, I will monitor progress on the planned separation, like the announcement of the new entity's leadership, initial registration statements (Form S-1 or Form 10), and any updates on the projected completion timeline. I will also scrutinize quarterly reports for updates on the integration of Core Ventures (Florida Cancer Specialists) and PRISM Vision.

Finally, I check whether McKesson is on track to complete its anticipated $2.5 billion in share repurchases for FY2026 (most likely to be done) and its continued annual dividend increases in line with earnings growth. Importantly, every MCK stock bull needs to check on any big legislative proposals related to drug pricing, mainly those targeting Medicare Part B reimbursement (ASP reform), as these can impact the profitability of the community oncology setting [neutralizing my main argument on bottom-line growth]. Also, any new material opioid-related lawsuits and adverse judgments can result in a stock price correction if financial charges exceed currently accrued liabilities.
